docs.title: Python OS Scheduler Simulator — v0.4
docs.text: A CLI tool that simulates CPU scheduling algorithms using Python. Built as a self-initiated learning project exploring OS internals.

docs.title: Algorithms
docs.text: Algorithm Type First-Come-First-Serve (FCFS) Non-preemptive Priority Scheduling Non-preemptive Round Robin (RR) Preemptive Shortest Job First (SJF) Non-preemptive

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Requires Python 3.x and Git.

git clone https://github.com/Kishore-M-r0502/OS_Projects.git
cd OS_Projects/scheduler

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
# Help
python scheduler_main.py -h

# FCFS
python scheduler_main.py --algo fcfs -p 5

# Priority
python scheduler_main.py --algorithm priority -p 8 -v

# Round Robin
python scheduler_main.py --algorithm rr -p 10 -q 5 -t 200 -v

# Autotrace
python scheduler_main.py --algorithm rr -p 20 -q 10 -t 200 -v --autotrace

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description -h Help CLI utility -a / --algorithm Algorithm to use (default: Round Robin) -p Process count -t Total scheduler running time (Round Robin) -q Per-process quantum (Round Robin) -v Verbose — human readable output -A / --autotrace Show internal state transitions -r / --route Redirect state transitions to a file

docs.title: About the Python OS Scheduler Simulator
What This Project Is
docs.text:
This is a simulator — not a real scheduler. It does not interact with the OS kernel or manage actual processes. What it does is model the decision-making logic of four CPU scheduling policies on randomly generated processes, and expose that logic through a flag-driven CLI.

The goal was to understand scheduling from the inside — by building it, not just reading about it.

docs.title: A Framework for Thinking About Schedulers
docs.text:
Most resources describe scheduling algorithms by mechanism. This project approaches them by classification.

Every scheduling algorithm can be understood through three views:

View Question Example Positional Who arrives first? FCFS ,SJF Access Who moves first? Priority Instance Who stays longer? Round Robin

This PAI framework — Positional, Access, Instance — is not standard terminology. It emerged from thinking about what actually differentiates one algorithm from another at a conceptual level, beyond just implementation details.

docs.title: Policy vs Algorithm
docs.text:
These two terms are often used interchangeably. This project treats them as distinct:

A Policy defines the rule of fairness or efficiency. It answers: what outcome are we optimizing for? If it cannot generate a baseline efficiency from its own requirements, it is not a policy — it is a situational response.

An Algorithm implements a policy. It answers: how do we achieve that outcome mechanically?

FCFS is both a policy (serve in arrival order) and an algorithm (maintain a queue, execute front-to-back). Round Robin is a policy (every process gets CPU time) implemented by an algorithm (quantum-based rotation).

docs.title: FCFS — First Come First Serve
docs.text: The simplest policy. Processes execute in arrival order, start to finish, with no interruption. Positional view — position in the queue determines everything.

docs.title: Priority Scheduling
docs.text: Processes are assigned a priority externally. The scheduler sorts by priority and executes in that order. Access view — who gets access is determined before execution begins.

docs.title: Round Robin
docs.text:
Each process runs for a fixed quantum. If it doesn't complete, it re-enters the queue. Repeats until all processes finish. Instance view — how long each process holds the CPU per instance is the defining constraint.

Known limitation: if the quantum exceeds total scheduler time, some processes never complete.

docs.title: Shortest Job First (SJF)
docs.text: The process with the shortest burst time runs first. Minimizes average waiting time but requires knowing burst times in advance — which in real systems, you rarely do.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The project has three components:

  • Process — a task with an identifier and burst time
  • Scheduler — generates execution order based on policy
  • Interface — controls what the user sees via flags

The flag system was designed deliberately. -v (verbose) and -A (autotrace) expose different layers of the simulation — human-readable output vs raw state transitions. -r redirects state transitions to a file, useful for inspecting longer runs.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: A simulator is a controlled environment for understanding something that is otherwise hidden inside an OS kernel. The value here is not the code — it is what the code makes visible.
